node_metadata.name: proximal myopathy with extrapyramidal signs
node_metadata.description: Proximal myopathy with extrapyramidal signs is a rare, hereditary non-dystrophic myopathy characterized by proximal muscle weakness, delayed motor development, learning difficulties, and progressive extrapyramidal motor signs including chorea, dystonia and tremor. Variable additional features have been reported - ataxia, microcephaly, ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, and optic atrophy.

node_metadata.name: CLDN14
node_metadata.description: Tight junctions represent one mode of cell-to-cell adhesion in epithelial or endothelial cell sheets, forming continuous seals around cells and serving as a physical barrier to prevent solutes and water from passing freely through the paracellular space. These junctions are comprised of sets of continuous networking strands in the outwardly facing cytoplasmic leaflet, with complementary grooves in the inwardly facing extracytoplasmic leaflet. The protein encoded by this gene, a member of the claudin family, is an integral membrane protein and a component of tight junction strands. The encoded protein also binds specifically to the WW domain of Yes-associated protein. Defects in this gene are the cause of an autosomal recessive form of nonsyndromic sensorineural deafness. It is also reported that four synonymous variants in this gene are associated with kidney stones and reduced bone mineral density. Several transcript variants encoding the same protein have been found for this gene. [provided by RefSeq, Jun 2010].

pathway_summary: This rare inherited muscle and movement disorder is noted to occur without hearing loss as part of its clinical presentation. Because hearing loss is a phenotype linked to a particular gene or protein, the pathway connects the disease to a molecular factor through the absence of that auditory feature.
explicit_query: In proximal myopathy with extrapyramidal signs, hearing impairment is a phenotype absent; which tight-junction claudin gene is associated with hearing impairment and is known to cause autosomal recessive nonsyndromic sensorineural deafness?
implicit_query: In proximal myopathy with extrapyramidal signs, the absence of hearing loss points away from defects in which tight-junction claudin gene that is classically associated with autosomal recessive nonsyndromic sensorineural deafness?
bare_query: Which gene encoding a tight junction membrane protein, also linked to autosomal recessive nonsyndromic sensorineural deafness, is associated with proximal myopathy with extrapyramidal signs?

node_metadata.description: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 36 (SCA36) is a subtype of autosomal dominant cerebellar ataxia type 1 (ADCA type 1) characterized by gait and limb ataxia, lower limb spasticity, dysarthria, muscle fasiculations, tongue atrophy and hyperreflexia. An autosomal dominant condition caused by mutation in the nop56 gene, encoding nucleolar protein 56. It is characterized by slowly progressive adult-onset gait ataxia, associated with eye movement abnormalities, tongue fasciculations and variable upper motor neuron signs. SCA36 presents in adulthood with ataxic dysarthria, truncal ataxia, limb ataxia, general hyperreflexia and variably occurring lower limb spasticity. Motor neuron involvement occurs later in the disease course and manifests with fasciculations and atrophy of the skeletal muscles and tongue. Sensorineural hearing loss may also be present in some. Oromandibular dystonia is a rare manifestation. Only mild cerebellar atrophy is reported.

node_metadata.name: MTHFR
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene catalyzes the conversion of 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate to 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, a co-substrate for homocysteine remethylation to methionine. Genetic variation in this gene influences susceptibility to occlusive vascular disease, neural tube defects, colon cancer and acute leukemia, and mutations in this gene are associated with methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase deficiency.[provided by RefSeq, Oct 2009].

pathway_summary: This hereditary cerebellar degeneration is characterized mainly by progressive ataxia, dysarthria, hyperreflexia, and later motor neuron involvement, while convulsive episodes are notably not part of its typical phenotype. Because the absence or presence of such neurologic features is often used to link disorders to underlying molecular causes, this pattern points toward an associated genetic or protein factor connected through the disease’s distinctive symptom profile.

node_metadata.description: An inherited giant platelet disorder with a complex phenotype characterized by congenital thrombocytopenia and possible subsequent manifestations of sensorineural hearing loss, presenile cataracts, elevation of liver enzymes, and/or progressive nephropathy often leading to end-stage renal disease (ESRD). Epstein syndrome, Fechtner syndrome, May-Hegglin anomaly and Sebastian syndrome, previously described as distinct disorders, represent some of the different clinical presentations of MYH9-RD. An autosomal dominant disorder characterized by thrombocytopenia, giant platelets, nephritis, and deafness; it is associated with mutation of the myh9 gene. Thrombocytopenia is present at birth and can be associated with spontaneous bleeding (usually mild or moderate). Bleeding symptoms include easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, and/or menorrhagia. Life-threatening hemorrhage is rare. Patients have an increased risk of hemorrhages after surgery, biopsy, or deliveries. Hearing loss is seen in almost all cases with the age of onset ranging from the 1st to 6th decade and the severity of hearing loss varying greatly (from mild defects to profound deafness). node_metadata.name: BCL2L1
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene belongs to the BCL-2 protein family. BCL-2 family members form hetero- or homodimers and act as anti- or pro-apoptotic regulators that are involved in a wide variety of cellular activities. The proteins encoded by this gene are located at the outer mitochondrial membrane, and have been shown to regulate outer mitochondrial membrane channel (VDAC) opening. VDAC regulates mitochondrial membrane potential, and thus controls the production of reactive oxygen species and release of cytochrome C by mitochondria, both of which are the potent inducers of cell apoptosis. Alternative splicing results in multiple transcript variants encoding two different isoforms. The longer isoform acts as an apoptotic inhibitor and the shorter isoform acts as an apoptotic activator. [provided by RefSeq, Dec 2015].

pathway_summary: This inherited giant-platelet disorder is characterized by congenital thrombocytopenia with leukocyte inclusions and may later develop kidney disease and progressive sensorineural deafness, although hearing deficits can be absent in some affected individuals. The link to a gene/protein is supported through its association with deafness, consistent with defects in a cytoskeletal motor protein whose mutation underlies this syndrome.
explicit_query: In macrothrombocytopenia and granulocyte inclusions with or without nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss, which gene/protein is associated with the phenotype absent of hearing impairment and encodes a BCL-2 family outer mitochondrial membrane regulator with anti- and pro-apoptotic isoforms?

node_metadata.name: spastic ataxia
node_metadata.description: Any autosomal dominant spastic ataxia in which the cause of the disease is a mutation in the VAMP1 gene. A group of inherited diseases that share similar phenotypes but are genetically diverse. Different genetic loci for autosomal recessive, autosomal dominant, and x-linked forms of hereditary spastic paraplegia have been identified. Clinically, patients present with slowly progressive distal limb weakness and lower extremity spasticity. Peripheral sensory neurons may be affected in the later stages of the disease. :61-6; Curr Opin Neurol 1997 Aug;10:313-8)

node_metadata.name: NPY
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a neuropeptide that is widely expressed in the central nervous system and influences many physiological processes, including cortical excitability, stress response, food intake, circadian rhythms, and cardiovascular function. The neuropeptide functions through G protein-coupled receptors to inhibit adenylyl cyclase, activate mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), regulate intracellular calcium levels, and activate potassium channels. A polymorphism in this gene resulting in a change of leucine 7 to proline in the signal peptide is associated with elevated cholesterol levels, higher alcohol consumption, and may be a risk factor for various metabolic and cardiovascular diseases. The protein also exhibits antimicrobial activity against bacteria and fungi. [provided by RefSeq, Oct 2014].

pathway_summary: This inherited movement disorder, caused by defects in a synaptic vesicle membrane protein, is characterized by progressive lower-limb stiffness and weakness. The pathway indicates that episodes of abnormal neuronal firing are not a feature of the condition, and that this neurological phenotype is used to connect the disorder to a relevant gene/protein association.

node_metadata.name: spinocerebellar ataxia
node_metadata.description: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 36 (SCA36) is a subtype of autosomal dominant cerebellar ataxia type 1 (ADCA type 1) characterized by gait and limb ataxia, lower limb spasticity, dysarthria, muscle fasiculations, tongue atrophy and hyperreflexia. An autosomal dominant condition caused by mutation in the nop56 gene, encoding nucleolar protein 56. It is characterized by slowly progressive adult-onset gait ataxia, associated with eye movement abnormalities, tongue fasciculations and variable upper motor neuron signs. SCA36 presents in adulthood with ataxic dysarthria, truncal ataxia, limb ataxia, general hyperreflexia and variably occurring lower limb spasticity. Motor neuron involvement occurs later in the disease course and manifests with fasciculations and atrophy of the skeletal muscles and tongue. Sensorineural hearing loss may also be present in some. Oromandibular dystonia is a rare manifestation. Only mild cerebellar atrophy is reported.

node_metadata.name: PIGP
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes an enzyme involved in the first step of glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI)-anchor biosynthesis. The GPI-anchor is a glycolipid found on many blood cells that serves to anchor proteins to the cell surface. The encoded protein is a component of the GPI-N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase complex that catalyzes the transfer of N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) from UDP-GlcNAc to phosphatidylinositol (PI). This gene is located in the Down Syndrome critical region on chromosome 21 and is a candidate for the pathogenesis of Down syndrome. This gene has multiple pseudogenes and is a member of the phosphatidylinositol glycan anchor biosynthesis gene family. Alternatively spliced transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been described. [provided by RefSeq, Feb 2016].

pathway_summary: This hereditary cerebellar degenerative disorder is characterized mainly by progressive ataxia, dysarthria, hyperreflexia, and later motor neuron involvement, while convulsive episodes are notably not part of its phenotype. The absence of that neurologic feature can still help connect the disease to a molecular factor, because such episodes are commonly used as a clinical sign associated with particular genes or proteins in neurologic disease biology.
explicit_query: Which gene, associated with seizure and encoding a component of the GPI-N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase complex involved in the first step of GPI-anchor biosynthesis, is implicated when seizure is a phenotype absent in spinocerebellar ataxia?
implicit_query: In spinocerebellar ataxia, which gene encoding a component of the GPI-N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase complex involved in the first step of GPI-anchor biosynthesis could be implicated, given that convulsive episodes are not part of the typical phenotype?
bare_query: Which gene involved in cell-surface protein anchoring is associated with spinocerebellar ataxia?

node_metadata.name: Coffin-Siris syndrome
node_metadata.description: Coffin-Siris syndrome 6

node_metadata.name: DBT
node_metadata.description: The branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex (BCKD) is an inner-mitochondrial enzyme complex involved in the breakdown of the branched-chain amino acids isoleucine, leucine, and valine. The BCKD complex is thought to be composed of a core of 24 transacylase (E2) subunits, and associated decarboxylase (E1), dehydrogenase (E3), and regulatory subunits. This gene encodes the transacylase (E2) subunit. Mutations in this gene result in maple syrup urine disease, type 2. Alternatively spliced transcript variants have been described, but their biological validity has not been determined. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: In this subtype of Coffin-Siris syndrome, seizures are notably absent as a clinical feature. Because seizure phenotypes are often used to connect neurodevelopmental disorders to underlying molecular causes, the lack of this neurologic manifestation helps characterize the associated causative gene or protein involved in the syndrome.
explicit_query: In Coffin-Siris syndrome, absence of the phenotype seizure is associated with which gene encoding the E2 transacylase subunit of the inner-mitochondrial branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex involved in isoleucine, leucine, and valine breakdown?
implicit_query: In the subtype of Coffin-Siris syndrome characterized by absence of seizures, which gene encoding the E2 transacylase subunit of the mitochondrial branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex is implicated?
bare_query: Which gene encoding a mitochondrial enzyme subunit involved in branched-chain amino acid metabolism is associated with Coffin-Siris syndrome?

node_metadata.name: autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia - pyramidal signs - nystagmus - oculomotor apraxia syndrome
node_metadata.description: Autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia-pyramidal signs-nystagmus-oculomotor apraxia syndrome is a rare, genetic, slowly progressive neurodegenerative disease characterized by delayed psychomotor development beginning in infancy, mild to profound intellectual disability, gait and stance ataxia, pyramidal signs (hyperreflexia, extensor plantar responses), dysarthria, and ocular abnormalities (e.g. nystagmus, oculomotor apraxia, abduction deficits, esotropia, ptosis). Brain imaging reveals progressive, generalized cerebellar atrophy, mild ventriculomegaly and, in some, retrocerebellar cysts.

node_metadata.name: SLC30A1
node_metadata.description: Predicted to enable calcium channel inhibitor activity and zinc ion transmembrane transporter activity. Predicted to be involved in several processes, including cellular divalent inorganic cation homeostasis; inorganic cation transmembrane transport; and negative regulation of transport. Predicted to act upstream of or within in utero embryonic development. Located in several cellular components, including Golgi apparatus; endoplasmic reticulum; and nuclear membrane. [provided by Alliance of Genome Resources, Apr 2022]

pathway_summary: This rare inherited neurodegenerative ataxia syndrome presents in infancy with delayed psychomotor development, intellectual disability, cerebellar gait and stance problems, pyramidal tract signs, dysarthria, and prominent eye movement abnormalities, alongside progressive cerebellar atrophy on imaging. In the phenotype profile of this disorder, epileptic events are specifically noted as absent, and this clinical feature is used in gene/protein association frameworks to help connect the syndrome to its underlying molecular cause.
explicit_query: In autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia - pyramidal signs - nystagmus - oculomotor apraxia syndrome, the phenotype absent is seizure; which gene associated with seizure is predicted to enable calcium channel inhibitor activity and zinc ion transmembrane transporter activity?
implicit_query: Which zinc transporter gene, also predicted to inhibit calcium channels and regulate cellular divalent cation homeostasis, has been associated with the rare infantile-onset autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia syndrome characterized by psychomotor delay, intellectual disability, pyramidal signs, nystagmus, oculomotor apraxia, progressive cerebellar atrophy, and notably no seizures?
bare_query: Which gene or protein involved in zinc ion transport and cellular divalent cation homeostasis is associated with autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia - pyramidal signs - nystagmus - oculomotor apraxia syndrome?

node_metadata.name: DDAH1
node_metadata.description: This gene belongs to the dimethylarginine dimethylaminohydrolase (DDAH) gene family. The encoded enzyme plays a role in nitric oxide generation by regulating cellular concentrations of methylarginines, which in turn inhibit nitric oxide synthase activity. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: Maleylacetoacetate isomerase deficiency is linked to impaired hepatic function, indicating that disruption of tyrosine catabolism can affect liver health. This connection suggests involvement of a molecular factor associated with reduced liver function, pointing to a gene or protein that contributes to maintaining normal hepatic activity in the context of this metabolic disorder.

node_metadata.name: neurofaciodigitorenal syndrome
node_metadata.description: Neurofaciodigitorenal syndrome is a rare, multiple developmental anomalies syndrome characterized by neurological abnormalities (including megalencephaly, hypotonia, intellectual disability, abnormal EEG), dysmorphic facial features (high prominent forehead, grooved nasal tip, ptosis, ear anomalies) and acrorenal defects (such as triphalangism, broad halluces, unilateral renal agenesis). Additionally, intrauterine growth restriction, short stature and congenital heart defects may be associated. There have been no further descriptions in the literature since 1997. A syndrome of multiple craniofacial anomalies , possible heart defect, finger and foot abnormalities, abnormal eeg, developmental delay, and kidney agenesis originally reported in one of two brothers..

node_metadata.name: PDYN
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene is a preproprotein that is proteolytically processed to form the secreted opioid peptides beta-neoendorphin, dynorphin, leu-enkephalin, rimorphin, and leumorphin. These peptides are ligands for the kappa-type of opioid receptor. Dynorphin is involved in modulating responses to several psychoactive substances, including cocaine. Multiple alternatively spliced transcript variants encoding the same protein have been found for this gene. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2010].

pathway_summary: This rare developmental disorder features abnormal brain development and EEG findings, but convulsive episodes are specifically reported as absent. Because the lack of this neurologic symptom is used in genotype–phenotype associations, the syndrome can be linked indirectly to a gene or protein through its relationship to that clinical feature.
explicit_query: In neurofaciodigitorenal syndrome, the phenotype absent of seizure is associated with which gene that encodes a preproprotein processed into secreted opioid peptides such as dynorphin and beta-neoendorphin?
implicit_query: In neurofaciodigitorenal syndrome, which gene encoding a precursor of dynorphin-related opioid peptides acting at kappa opioid receptors is indirectly implicated through the reported absence of convulsive episodes?
bare_query: Which gene involved in producing several secreted opioid peptides linked to kappa-type opioid receptor signaling is associated with neurofaciodigitorenal syndrome?

node_metadata.name: osteogenesis imperfecta
node_metadata.description: Any osteogenesis imperfecta in which the cause of the disease is a mutation in the BMP1 gene. collagen diseases characterized by brittle, osteoporotic, and easily fractured bones. It may also present with blue sclerae, loose joints, and imperfect dentin formation. Most types are autosomal dominant and are associated with mutations in collagen type i.

node_metadata.name: HSD17B4
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene is a bifunctional enzyme that is involved in the peroxisomal beta-oxidation pathway for fatty acids. It also acts as a catalyst for the formation of 3-ketoacyl-CoA intermediates from both straight-chain and 2-methyl-branched-chain fatty acids. Defects in this gene that affect the peroxisomal fatty acid beta-oxidation activity are a cause of D-bifunctional protein deficiency (DBPD). An apparent pseudogene of this gene is present on chromosome 8. Multiple alternatively spliced transcript variants encoding distinct isoforms have been found for this gene. [provided by RefSeq, May 2014].

pathway_summary: A form of brittle bone disease caused by defects in matrix-processing pathways can present without loss of hearing, a clinical feature often considered in connective tissue disorders. Because hearing loss is a phenotype linked to specific molecular factors, this pathway connects the disorder to a gene or protein associated with auditory dysfunction.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with hearing impairment, a phenotype absent in osteogenesis imperfecta, and encodes a bifunctional peroxisomal beta-oxidation enzyme that catalyzes formation of 3-ketoacyl-CoA intermediates from straight-chain and 2-methyl-branched-chain fatty acids?
implicit_query: In osteogenesis imperfecta caused by defects in matrix-processing pathways and presenting without hearing loss, which peroxisomal bifunctional fatty acid beta-oxidation enzyme linked to auditory dysfunction should be considered?
bare_query: Which gene encoding a bifunctional peroxisomal enzyme involved in fatty acid metabolism is linked to osteogenesis imperfecta?

triplets: macrothrombocytopenia and granulocyte inclusions with or without nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss

topic_entities.27475: macrothrombocytopenia and granulocyte inclusions with or without nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss

node_metadata.name: macrothrombocytopenia and granulocyte inclusions with or without nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss
node_metadata.description: An inherited giant platelet disorder with a complex phenotype characterized by congenital thrombocytopenia and possible subsequent manifestations of sensorineural hearing loss, presenile cataracts, elevation of liver enzymes, and/or progressive nephropathy often leading to end-stage renal disease (ESRD). Epstein syndrome, Fechtner syndrome, May-Hegglin anomaly and Sebastian syndrome, previously described as distinct disorders, represent some of the different clinical presentations of MYH9-RD. An autosomal dominant disorder characterized by thrombocytopenia, giant platelets, nephritis, and deafness; it is associated with mutation of the myh9 gene. Thrombocytopenia is present at birth and can be associated with spontaneous bleeding (usually mild or moderate). Bleeding symptoms include easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, and/or menorrhagia. Life-threatening hemorrhage is rare. Patients have an increased risk of hemorrhages after surgery, biopsy, or deliveries. Hearing loss is seen in almost all cases with the age of onset ranging from the 1st to 6th decade and the severity of hearing loss varying greatly (from mild defects to profound deafness). Proteinuric nephropathy occurs in about 30% of patients and in 75% is diagnosed before the age of 35. The majority of patients with kidney damage progress to ESRD within a few years. Patients that develop kidney involvement later in life often have a slower progression towards ESRD. Cataracts occur in about 20% of patients, are often bilateral and usually appear in early adulthood, but congenital cases have been reported.

node_metadata.name: BTD
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene functions to recycle protein-bound biotin by cleaving biocytin (biotin-epsilon-lysine), a normal product of carboxylase degradation, resulting in regeneration of free biotin. The encoded protein has also been shown to have biotinyl transferase activity. Mutations in this gene are associated with biotinidase deficiency. Multiple transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been described. [provided by RefSeq, Aug 2013].

pathway_summary: This inherited giant-platelet disorder is notable for congenital thrombocytopenia and leukocyte inclusions, with some patients later developing kidney disease and inner-ear dysfunction. The pathway indicates that the hearing-related feature linked to this syndrome is associated with a specific gene/protein, consistent with the known molecular basis of this autosomal dominant platelet disorder.
explicit_query: In macrothrombocytopenia and granulocyte inclusions with or without nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss, the phenotype absent is hearing impairment; which gene associated with hearing impairment encodes a protein that recycles protein-bound biotin by cleaving biocytin to regenerate free biotin and also has biotinyl transferase activity?
implicit_query: In the inherited giant-platelet syndrome characterized by congenital thrombocytopenia, leukocyte inclusions, and sometimes nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss, which gene encodes the enzyme responsible for recycling protein-bound biotin by cleaving biocytin?
bare_query: Which gene involved in recycling protein-bound biotin and associated with biotinidase deficiency is also linked to macrothrombocytopenia and granulocyte inclusions with or without nephritis or sensorineural hearing loss?

node_metadata.name: Fenretinide
node_metadata.description: A synthetic retinoid that is used orally as a chemopreventive against prostate cancer and in women at risk of developing contralateral breast cancer. It is also effective as an antineoplastic agent. Fenretinide inhibits the growth of several human cancer cell lines, acting through both retinoid-receptor-dependent and retinoid-receptor-independent mechanisms.1In vivo, fenretinide selectively accumulates in breast tissue and is particularly active in inhibiting rat mammary carcinogenesis.1 An important feature of fenretinide is its ability to inhibit cell growth through the induction of apoptosis rather than through differentiation, an effect that is strikingly different from that of vitamin A.1 In contrast to tamoxifen, which inhibits only estrogen receptor (ER)-positive tumors, fenretinide induces apoptosis in both ER-positive and ER-negative breast cancer cell lines.2 All of these properties render fenretinide an attractive candidate for breast cancer chemoprevention. Investigated for use/treatment in macular degeneration.

node_metadata.name: Vitamin A
node_metadata.description:
Retinol and derivatives of retinol that play an essential role in metabolic functioning of the retina, the growth of and differentiation of epithelial tissue, the growth of bone, reproduction, and the immune response. Dietary vitamin A is derived from a variety of carotenoids found in plants. It is enriched in the liver, egg yolks, and the fat component of dairy products. Vision:Vitamin A (all-trans retinol) is converted in the retina to the 11-cis-isomer of retinaldehyde or 11-cis-retinal. 11-cis-retinal functions in the retina in the transduction of light into the neural signals necessary for vision. 11-cis-retinal, while attached to opsin in rhodopsin is isomerized to all-trans-retinal by light. This is the event that triggers the nerve impulse to the brain which allows for the perception of light. All-trans-retinal is then released from opsin and reduced to all-trans-retinol. All-trans-retinol is isomerized to 11-cis-retinol in the dark, and then oxidized to 11-cis-retinal. 11-cis-retinal recombines with opsin to re-form rhodopsin. Night blindness or defective vision at low illumination results from a failure to re-synthesize 11-cis retinal rapidly.
Epithelial differentiation: The role of Vitamin A in epithelial differentiation, as well as in other physiological processes, involves the binding of Vitamin A to two families of nuclear retinoid receptors (retinoic acid receptors, RARs; and retinoid-X receptors, RXRs). These receptors function as ligand-activated transcription factors that modulate gene transcription. When there is not enough Vitamin A to bind these receptors, natural cell differentiation and growth are interrupted. For the treatment of vitamin A deficiency.

node_metadata.name: RDH11
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene is an NADPH-dependent retinal reductase and a short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase. The encoded protein has no steroid dehydrogenase activity. [provided by RefSeq, Nov 2011]

pathway_summary: Fenretinide, a synthetic retinoid with anticancer activity, has a synergistic relationship with vitamin A biology. Vitamin A exerts its downstream effects by binding nuclear retinoid receptors that act as ligand-activated transcription factors, thereby regulating gene expression. Thus, fenretinide is indirectly connected to a gene/protein-level target through cooperation with this retinoid signaling system.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is targeted by Vitamin A in the synergistic interaction pathway involving Fenretinide, and is described as an NADPH-dependent retinal reductase and short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase with no steroid dehydrogenase activity?
implicit_query: Which NADPH-dependent retinal reductase in the short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase family, lacking steroid dehydrogenase activity, could be indirectly linked to fenretinide through cooperation with vitamin A–responsive nuclear transcriptional signaling?
bare_query: Which NADPH-dependent retinal reductase in the short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase family is associated with Fenretinide?

node_metadata.name: autosomal recessive congenital ichthyosis
node_metadata.description: Any autosomal recessive congenital ichthyosis in which the cause of the disease is a mutation in the ABCA12 gene. A chronic, congenital ichthyosis inherited as an autosomal recessive trait. Infants are usually born encased in a collodion membrane which sheds within a few weeks. Scaling is generalized and marked with grayish-brown quadrilateral scales, adherent at their centers and free at the edges. In some cases, scales are so thick that they resemble armored plate.

node_metadata.name: LIPN
node_metadata.description: The gene encodes a lipase that is highly expressed in granular keratinocytes in the epidermis, and plays a role in the differentiation of keratinocytes. Mutations in this gene are associated with lamellar ichthyosis type 4. [provided by RefSeq, Dec 2011].

pathway_summary: This congenital erythrodermic ichthyosis is a subtype within the broader group of inherited keratinization disorders that present at birth with generalized scaling, often after a collodion membrane. The broader disorder category is linked to defects in a lipid transporter involved in epidermal barrier formation, indicating that this skin disease can be connected to a causative gene/protein in that pathway.

node_metadata.name: cleft lip/palate
node_metadata.description: Cleft lip and palate is a fissure type embryopathy extending across the upper lip, nasal base, alveolar ridge and the hard and soft palate. Cleft lip and cleft palate are birth defects that occur when a baby's lip or mouth do not form properly. They happen early during pregnancy. A baby can have a cleft lip, a cleft palate, or both. A cleft lip happens if the tissue that makes up the lip does not join completely before birth. This causes an opening in the upper lip. The opening can be a small slit or a large opening that goes through the lip into the nose. It can be on one or both sides of the lip or, rarely, in the middle of the lip. Children with a cleft lip also can have a cleft palate. The roof of the mouth is called the \palate.\" With a cleft palate, the tissue that makes up the roof of the mouth does not join correctly. Babies may have both the front and back parts of the palate open, or they may have only one part open. Children with a cleft lip or a cleft palate often have problems with feeding and talking. They also might have ear infections, hearing loss, and problems with their teeth. Often, surgery can close the lip and palate. Cleft lip surgery is usually done before age 12 months, and cleft palate surgery is done before 18 months. Many children have other complications. They may need additional surgeries, dental and orthodontic care, and speech therapy as they get older. With treatment, most children with clefts do well and lead a healthy life. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention" The malformation is the association, in varying degrees, of cleft lip/alveolus and cleft palate (see these terms). The lip and alveolus anomaly is paramedian and is located at the philtrum level for the lip and at the level of the upper lateral incisors for the alveolar ridge. It involves a cutaneous, muscular and mucosal interruption in the lip in addition to a nostril and nasal septum deformity and a gap in the alveolar bone and dental arch. Clinical forms range from a simple midline groove in the lip to a complete cleft lip and palate reaching the incisive foramen with an opening in the base of the nostril. The cleft seen in cleft palate is median. The clinical forms range from a simple bifid uvula (see this term) to a complete cleft palate reaching the incisive foramen. Cleft palate can disrupt sucking-swallowing in newborns. In addition, Eustachian tube malformation can lead to incomplete drainage of the middle ear with recurrent otitis and transmission hypoacousia. The maxillary lateral incisor, at the site of the alveolar cleft, can present with anomalies in shape, number (duplication or agenesis) and position. There is no correlation seen between temporary (primary) and permanent dentition.

node_metadata.name: cleft palate with or without ankyloglossia, X-linked
node_metadata.description: X-linked cleft palate and ankyloglossia is a rare, genetic developmental defect during embryogenesis syndrome characterized by the association of complete, partial or submucous cleft palate and ankyloglossia. Patients may also present abnormal uvula (e.g. absent, bifid, shortened or laterally deviated), short lingual frenulum and dental anomalies (e.g. buccal crossbite, absent and/or misshapen teeth). Digital abnormalities, such as mild clinodactyly and/or syndactyly, have also been reported. Congenital fissure of the soft and/or hard palate, due to faulty fusion.

node_metadata.name: NOS3
node_metadata.description: Nitric oxide is a reactive free radical which acts as a biologic mediator in several processes, including neurotransmission and antimicrobial and antitumoral activities. Nitric oxide is synthesized from L-arginine by nitric oxide synthases. Variations in this gene are associated with susceptibility to coronary spasm. Alternative splicing and the use of alternative promoters results in multiple transcript variants. [provided by RefSeq, Oct 2016].

pathway_summary: A broad craniofacial birth-defect spectrum involving failure of fusion of the upper lip and/or palate includes a rarer inherited form marked by palatal clefting together with tongue-tie and other oral anomalies. Because this specific subtype is X-linked and genetically determined, it points to an underlying causative gene or protein involved in embryonic midline craniofacial development, especially formation of the palate and oral structures.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with cleft palate with or without ankyloglossia, X-linked, a parent-child subtype of cleft lip/palate, and encodes a nitric oxide synthase that produces nitric oxide from L-arginine?
implicit_query: Which nitric oxide synthase gene, responsible for producing a biologic mediator from L-arginine, has been implicated in genetic susceptibility to cleft lip and palate, including inherited craniofacial forms affecting palatal and oral development?
bare_query: Which gene involved in nitric oxide synthesis and biologic mediator functions is associated with cleft lip/palate?

node_metadata.name: juvenile-onset diabetes mellitus-central and peripheral neurodegeneration syndrome
node_metadata.description: Diabetes symptoms vary depending on how much your blood sugar is elevated. Some people, especially those with prediabetes or type 2 diabetes, may sometimes not experience symptoms. In type 1 diabetes, symptoms tend to come on quickly and be more severe. Some of the signs and symptoms of type 1 diabetes and type 2 diabetes are: Increased thirst, Frequent urination, Extreme hunger, Unexplained weight loss, Presence of ketones in the urine, Fatigue, Irritability, Blurred vision, Slow-healing sores, Frequent infections, such as gums or skin infections and vaginal infections, Type 1 diabetes can develop at any age, though it often appears during childhood or adolescence. Type 2 diabetes, the more common type, can develop at any age, though it's more common in people older than 40.

node_metadata.name: diabetes mellitus (disease)
node_metadata.description: A metabolic disorder characterized by abnormally high blood sugar levels due to diminished production of insulin or insulin resistance/desensitization. A heterogeneous group of disorders characterized by hyperglycemia and glucose intolerance.

node_metadata.name: ICAM1
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a cell surface glycoprotein which is typically expressed on endothelial cells and cells of the immune system. It binds to integrins of type CD11a / CD18, or CD11b / CD18 and is also exploited by Rhinovirus as a receptor. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: This rare childhood syndrome is a specific subtype of diabetes characterized by abnormal elevation of blood glucose from impaired insulin production or action. Because it falls under the broader category of hyperglycemic metabolic disorders, it is linked through that parent disease class to genes or proteins associated with glucose regulation, insulin signaling, and related metabolic dysfunction.
explicit_query: Which cell surface glycoprotein, typically expressed on endothelial and immune cells and binding the integrins CD11a/CD18 and CD11b/CD18, is associated with diabetes mellitus, the parent disease of juvenile-onset diabetes mellitus-central and peripheral neurodegeneration syndrome?
implicit_query: In juvenile-onset diabetes mellitus-central and peripheral neurodegeneration syndrome, which endothelial and immune cell surface glycoprotein involved in leukocyte integrin binding and also known to serve as a rhinovirus receptor may be implicated through broader hyperglycemic metabolic disease mechanisms?
bare_query: Which endothelial and immune cell surface glycoprotein, also known as a receptor used by rhinovirus, is associated with juvenile-onset diabetes mellitus-central and peripheral neurodegeneration syndrome?

node_metadata.name: spondylometaphyseal dysplasia
node_metadata.description: spondylometaphyseal dysplasia, East African type

node_metadata.name: spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia
node_metadata.description: A very rare type of spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia described in fewer than 10 patients to date and characterized clinically by dysplastic epiphyses, short stature appearing in infancy, short neck, short and stubby hands and feet, scoliosis, genu valgum, abnormal pelvis, osteoporosis and osteoarthritis. A very rare type of spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia described in fewer than 10 patients to date with clinical characteristics of dysplastic epiphyses, short stature appearing in infancy, short neck, short and stubby hands and feet, scoliosis, genu valgum, abnormal pelvis, osteoporosis and osteoarthritis.

node_metadata.name: CYP7B1
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a member of the cytochrome P450 superfamily of enzymes. The cytochrome P450 proteins are monooxygenases which catalyze many reactions involved in drug metabolism and synthesis of cholesterol, steroids and other lipids. This endoplasmic reticulum membrane protein catalyzes the first reaction in the cholesterol catabolic pathway of extrahepatic tissues, which converts cholesterol to bile acids. This enzyme likely plays a minor role in total bile acid synthesis, but may also be involved in the development of atherosclerosis, neurosteroid metabolism and sex hormone synthesis. Mutations in this gene have been associated with hereditary spastic paraplegia (SPG5 or HSP), an autosomal recessive disorder. [provided by RefSeq, Apr 2016].

pathway_summary: The East African form of this skeletal dysplasia is classified within a broader group of vertebral and long-bone growth disorders. That broader syndrome is an extremely rare osteochondrodysplasia marked by infant-onset short stature, abnormal epiphyses, short neck, stubby hands and feet, scoliosis, genu valgum, pelvic abnormalities, osteoporosis, and early osteoarthritis, and it has been linked to an underlying gene/protein.
explicit_query: Which gene is associated with spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia, the parent-child disorder category that includes spondylometaphyseal dysplasia, and encodes an endoplasmic reticulum cytochrome P450 monooxygenase involved in cholesterol catabolism to bile acids?
implicit_query: Which gene encodes an endoplasmic reticulum cytochrome P450 enzyme that initiates extrahepatic cholesterol catabolism toward bile acid synthesis and has been implicated in the broader syndrome that includes the East African form of spondylometaphyseal dysplasia?
bare_query: Which gene encoding a cytochrome P450 family protein involved in cholesterol and lipid metabolism is associated with spondylometaphyseal dysplasia?

node_metadata.name: spastic paraplegia-nephritis-deafness syndrome
node_metadata.description: This syndrome is characterised by variable spastic paraplegia, bilateral sensorineural deafness, intellectual deficit and progressive nephropathy.

node_metadata.name: syndromic genetic deafness
node_metadata.description: A bilateral form of sensorineural hearing impairment.

node_metadata.name: OTOF
node_metadata.description: Mutations in this gene are a cause of neurosensory nonsyndromic recessive deafness, DFNB9. The short form of the encoded protein has 3 C2 domains, a single carboxy-terminal transmembrane domain found also in the C. elegans spermatogenesis factor FER-1 and human dysferlin, while the long form has 6 C2 domains. The homology suggests that this protein may be involved in vesicle membrane fusion. Several transcript variants encoding multiple isoforms have been found for this gene. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: This rare inherited disorder, marked by spastic weakness of the legs, bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, cognitive impairment, and progressive kidney disease, falls within the broader category of hereditary deafness syndromes. Because that broader class is linked to specific genes and their protein products, the syndrome is therefore connected to a gene/protein entity through its membership in this syndromic form of genetic hearing impairment.
explicit_query: Which gene is associated with syndromic genetic deafness in the parent-child classification of spastic paraplegia-nephritis-deafness syndrome and is known for mutations causing neurosensory nonsyndromic recessive deafness, DFNB9?
implicit_query: Which gene involved in vesicle membrane fusion and known for causing autosomal recessive nonsyndromic sensorineural deafness (DFNB9) is linked to spastic paraplegia-nephritis-deafness syndrome through its classification among hereditary deafness syndromes?
